title: QueryForge
description: A complete relational database engine — lexer, Pratt parser, a statistics-driven cost-based optimizer with cost-based join reordering (Selinger-style subset DP), index-only (covering) scans, bitmap-AND of multiple indexes and composite B+Tree indexes, a Volcano-model executor with hash & sort-merge joins, an external merge sort, GROUPING SETS/ROLLUP/CUBE with GROUPING(), ordered-set aggregates (PERCENTILE_CONT/DISC, MODE), correlated subqueries, CTEs (incl. WITH RECURSIVE), set operations, standard-grade window functions, statistical aggregates with FILTER, transactions, CSV import, an SVG chart view and an EXPLAIN visualizer. First-class temporal types — DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP and INTERVAL as tagged values that index, sort, group and join like any column — with typed literals, calendar-aware date arithmetic (month-clamping), EXTRACT/DATE_TRUNC/AGE, MAKE_* constructors and CURRENT_DATE/TIMESTAMP. Full declarative integrity: PRIMARY KEY/UNIQUE (composite), CHECK and DEFAULT, and FOREIGN KEYs with ON DELETE/UPDATE referential actions (CASCADE/RESTRICT/SET NULL/SET DEFAULT, recursively), ALTER TABLE schema evolution, and atomic statements that roll back wholesale on any constraint failure. And first-class exact numerics: arbitrary-precision DECIMAL/NUMERIC (BigInt-backed) where 0.1 + 0.2 is exactly 0.3 and money SUMs never lose a cent — DECIMAL(p,s) columns, exact arithmetic & aggregates, and Postgres-style TO_CHAR currency formatting. Plus VIEWs (CREATE [OR REPLACE] VIEW, inlined wherever they're used, even inside other views), UPSERT (INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING / DO UPDATE with EXCLUDED), and an optimizer that decorrelates a correlated [NOT] EXISTS into a single hash SemiJoin / AntiJoin instead of re-running the subquery per row. And now first-class JSON (jsonb-style): a tagged, normalized value (sorted/de-duplicated keys) that indexes, sorts, groups, joins and persists like any column, with the path/containment operators (-> ->> #> #>> @> <@ ?), a Postgres ::TYPE postfix cast, a scalar/aggregate library (JSON_BUILD_OBJECT/ARRAY, JSON_EXTRACT_PATH, JSONB_SET, JSON_PRETTY, JSON_AGG, JSON_OBJECT_AGG, …) and set-returning table functions in FROM (JSON_ARRAY_ELEMENTS, JSON_EACH, …) that unnest JSON into rows. And now first-class FULL-TEXT SEARCH (Postgres tsvector/tsquery): two more tagged values threaded through the same value plumbing so a search document indexes, sorts, groups, joins and persists like any column — built on a from-scratch Porter (1980) stemmer, an English stop-word list and a positional tokenizer. The @@ match operator does boolean (& | !), prefix (:*), weight-filtered (:A) and true positional PHRASE search (a <-> b, a <N> b) via a position-set executor; to_tsvector / to_tsquery / plainto_tsquery / phraseto_tsquery / websearch_to_tsquery build documents and queries; ts_rank / ts_rank_cd rank by A/B/C/D weights and cover density; ts_headline highlights; setweight / strip / || shape vectors. The capstone is a real GIN inverted index (CREATE INDEX … USING GIN) — maintained on every insert/update/delete and across snapshots — that the planner turns into a posting-list candidate probe plus an exact recheck (a GinScan in EXPLAIN), so search is sublinear yet byte-for-byte identical to the sequential-scan filter. And now STANDARD-GRADE WINDOW FUNCTIONS: a from-scratch frame engine with all three modes — ROWS (physical), RANGE (typed value offsets over numbers, exact DECIMAL and DATE/TIMESTAMP ± INTERVAL, honouring ASC/DESC) and GROUPS (peer groups) — each with the EXCLUDE clause (NO OTHERS / CURRENT ROW / GROUP / TIES), aggregate FILTER (WHERE …) in the frame, IGNORE NULLS / RESPECT NULLS value functions (FIRST_VALUE/LAST_VALUE/NTH_VALUE/LAG/LEAD), the statistical (STDDEV/VARIANCE) and ordered-set (PERCENTILE_CONT/DISC, MODE) families as windows, and a named WINDOW clause with reference inheritance (OVER (w …)) — all proven against the GROUP BY aggregates in self-tests. And now first-class ARRAY types (T[]): a fifth tagged value — nestable, so multi-dimensional — threaded through the same value plumbing so an array column indexes, sorts, groups, joins and persists like any other, with a Postgres element-wise total order. The ARRAY[…] constructor and {…} text literals build them; 1-based subscripts a[i] and inclusive slices a[lo:hi] read them; the containment/overlap operators (@> <@ &&) and the array-operand quantifiers (x = ANY(a), x < ALL(a)) test them with full three-valued logic; || concatenates (array‖array, array‖elem, elem‖array). A full function library — array_length / cardinality / array_ndims / array_dims, array_append / prepend / cat / remove / replace, array_position(s) / trim_array, array_to_string / string_to_array — plus the array_agg aggregate (ordered, NULL-keeping, DISTINCT) and the set-returning unnest() / generate_subscripts() table functions (so LATERAL unnest(t.col) expands a column into rows), with to_json / ::json interop. The capstone is a GIN inverted index over an array column (CREATE INDEX … USING GIN) — maintained on every insert/update/delete and across snapshots — that the planner turns into a posting-list candidate probe (AND for @>, OR for && / = ANY) plus an exact recheck (a GinScan in EXPLAIN), so array search is sublinear yet byte-for-byte identical to the sequential-scan filter. And now a from-scratch MVCC CONCURRENCY ENGINE with an interactive Concurrency Lab: per-key version chains (xmin/xmax) with snapshot visibility, the four isolation levels (READ UNCOMMITTED → SERIALIZABLE) including Serializable Snapshot Isolation (Cahill's rw-antidependency graph with the PostgreSQL dangerous-structure pivot rule), first-updater-wins write conflicts, lock-wait blocking with waits-for deadlock detection, and a deterministic schedule runner that drives canonical anomaly scenarios (dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom, lost update, write skew, deadlock, the read-only anomaly) — visualized as a transaction timeline, a live version-chain inspector, an rw-conflict graph and a serializability verdict, so you watch each anomaly appear and vanish as you raise the isolation level. And now a LEVELLED-UP COST-BASED OPTIMIZER with an interactive Optimizer Lab: a System-R distinct-value join-cardinality model (|L|·|R| / max(V(L),V(R)), with each key's distinct count capped by its input's surviving rows, so a selective filter finally propagates through a join), an INDEX NESTED-LOOP JOIN (a tiny outer driver probes a large inner table's B+Tree on the join key — one index descent per outer row instead of hashing the whole inner side, chosen by cost), and a what-if INDEX ADVISOR (HypoPG / SQL-Server-DTA style) that enumerates candidate indexes from a query's equalities, ranges, join keys and ORDER BY, builds each one hypothetically (a real backfilled B+Tree, retracted the instant the plan is costed — your data is never touched), re-plans, and recommends only the indexes the optimizer would actually adopt at a lower cost, each with a ready-to-run CREATE INDEX and one-click Apply. The Optimizer Lab makes it legible — the chosen plan with per-operator cost, a replay of the join-order subset-DP search (every relation subset, its cheapest sub-plan, the order it settled on), and the advisor's ranked recommendations (including an index that flips a hash join into a cheaper index nested-loop join). And now a from-scratch ARIES WRITE-AHEAD LOGGING & CRASH-RECOVERY engine with an interactive Recovery Lab: single-cell pages with pageLSNs, a forced on-disk log vs. a volatile tail, a STEAL/NO-FORCE buffer pool (so a crash needs both REDO and UNDO), the write-ahead rule on page flush, log-forcing commits and fuzzy checkpoints — recovered by the canonical three passes (Analysis rebuilds the dirty-page/transaction tables and the RedoLSN; Redo repeats history, winners and losers alike, idempotently via the pageLSN test; Undo rolls losers back in reverse-LSN order logging redo-only CLRs with undoNextLSN, so a crash *during* recovery resumes without double-undoing). The Recovery Lab scrubs a workload → crash → recovery as one timeline — a phase rail, a live log (durable/volatile/recovery-written), disk+buffer page images, the rebuilt tables, and a verdict that checks the recovered state against an independently computed correct outcome. And now MEMORY-BOUNDED EXECUTION with an interactive Execution Lab: a SET work_mem row-budget knob that makes every blocking operator spill to "disk" instead of materializing an unbounded working set — a top-N heapsort (ORDER BY … LIMIT keeps only the top k rows in a bounded heap, provably identical to a full sort then slice), a work_mem-bounded external merge sort, a grace HASH AGGREGATE (overflow group keys are hash-partitioned and spilled, then each partition re-aggregated — so a huge GROUP BY stays exact, COUNT(DISTINCT) and all), and a grace HASH JOIN (both inputs partitioned by the join-key hash and joined partition-by-partition, NULL keys streamed separately so every outer-join flavour is preserved), all recursing on skew. EXPLAIN ANALYZE carries per-operator memory accounting (peak rows, spilled rows, partitions, passes); the Execution Lab drags a work_mem slider and shows the plan switch from in-memory to spilling with live memory bars, while a side-by-side unbounded run proves the answer is identical multiset for multiset. Plus a safe statement (parse) cache for repeated read-only queries. And now a SECOND, INDEPENDENT EXECUTION ENGINE — a from-scratch VECTORIZED (columnar) executor with an interactive Vectorize Lab: the whole database runs the Volcano (row-at-a-time iterator) model, the textbook slow path for analytics, so the analytic subset (single table; numeric WHERE / GROUP BY / aggregates) also runs the MonetDB/X100 → DuckDB way — a relation transposed once into packed Float64Array columns, batches of a column pushed through type-specialized kernels compiled as closures over the captured typed arrays (col[i] directly — no boxed SqlValue, no generic comparator), a filter narrowing an Int32Array selection vector instead of copying rows, and a GROUP BY keyed on the numeric tuple by a native open-addressing hash table (no per-row string key) accumulating COUNT/SUM/AVG/MIN/MAX into flat typed arrays, order-preservingly so even a floating-point SUM matches the row engine bit-for-bit. A conservative analyzer runs only what it can prove it matches and falls back to Volcano otherwise; a differential self-test group runs queries through both engines asserting identical multisets; and the Vectorize Lab races them live over a large dataset — an identical-result verdict, Volcano-vs-vectorized timing and throughput bars, the speedup factor (20×+ on group-by roll-ups), and a vector-width sweep chart showing the cache-residency sweet spot. Proven by 438 in-app self-tests. Built from scratch in TypeScript, running entirely in the browser.
